---
title: "Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis in Adults: A Systematic Review (2016-2026)"
subtitle: "A Systematic Literature Review"
date: "2026-03-25"
author: "Automated Literature Review Pipeline"
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    cite-method: citeproc
    documentclass: article
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    linestretch: 1.5
  docx:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
bibliography: references.bib
csl: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/citation-style-language/styles/master/apa.csl
abstract: |
  **Background:** Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) is a life-threatening hyperinflammatory syndrome characterized by uncontrolled immune activation, excessive cytokine release, and multi-organ damage. In adults, HLH is most often secondary to infections, malignancies, and autoimmune conditions, yet remains underrecognized with high mortality. The expanding use of chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy and immune checkpoint inhibitors, together with COVID-19-associated hyperinflammation, has renewed interest in HLH pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management.
  **Methods:** A systematic search was conducted across Scopus, PubMed, and Embase using predefined terms related to hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis, macrophage activation syndrome, cytokine storm, and hyperinflammation in adults. Records were screened following PRISMA guidelines. A journal quality threshold of CiteScore 3.0 or higher was applied to ensure inclusion of high-impact evidence.
  **Results:** The search identified 897 records, of which 783 remained after deduplication. Following screening, 50 articles across 36 journals met inclusion criteria, covering the period 2016 to 2021 with an average of 945 citations per article. Key advances include refined diagnostic tools (HLH-2004, HScore, MAS criteria), emerging iatrogenic triggers (CAR-T, checkpoint inhibitors), and targeted therapies (emapalumab, anakinra, ruxolitinib).
  **Conclusions:** The adult HLH literature reflects a rapidly evolving field. Diagnostic criteria remain imperfect for adult populations, and prospective trials are urgently needed. Future research should prioritize adult-specific diagnostic algorithms, biomarker-guided treatment protocols, and standardized outcome measures.
---

{{< include sections/01-introduction.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/02-methods.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/03-pathogenesis.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/04-diagnosis.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/05-etiology.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/06-treatment.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/07-covid.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/08-discussion.qmd >}}

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
